perturbation.original_text: For the services to be rendered and facilities to be provided by the Sponsor hereunder, the Trust shall pay Domini a fee accrued daily and payable monthly at an annual rate equal to 0.50% of the Trust's average daily net assets for the Trust's then current fiscal year.
perturbation.changed_text: For the services to be rendered and facilities to be provided by the Sponsor hereunder, the Trust shall pay Domini a fee accrued daily and payable annually at an annual rate equal to 0.50% of the Trust's average daily net assets for the Trust's then current fiscal year.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment frequency from monthly to annually could create cash flow issues for Domini, and delay in payments like this may violate regulations related to timely payment for services rendered.
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts General Law Chapter 149 Section 148 requires prompt and regular payment of wages. While this typically applies to employees, extending the payment period to annually for services rendered under this agreement could be construed as a violation, especially if Domini is dependent on the regular payments for its operational expenses. By changing to an annual payment, the law's intention of protecting workers/service providers from delayed and infrequent payments is contradicted, even if Domini is technically a company and not an employee.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XXI, Chapter 149, Section 148 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XXI LABOR AND INDUSTRIES Chapter 149 LABOR AND INDUSTRIES Section 148 Payment of wages; commissions; exemption by contract; persons deemed employers; provision for cashing check or draft; violation of statute Section 148 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 148: Payment of wages; commissions; exemption by contract; persons deemed employers; provision for cashing check or draft; violation of statute Section 148. Every person having employees in his service shall pay weekly or bi-weekly each such employee the wages earned by him to within six days of the termination of the pay period during which the wages were earned if employed for five or six days in a calendar week, or to within seven days of the termination of the pay period during which the wages were earned if such employee is employed seven days in a calendar week, or in the case of an employee who has worked for a period of less than five days, hereinafter called a casual employee, shall, within seven days after the termination of such period, pay the wages earned by such casual employee during such period, but any employee leaving his employment shall be paid in full on the following regular pay day, and, in the absence of a regular pay day, on the following Saturday; and any employee discharged from such employment shall be paid in full on the day of his discharge, or in Boston as soon as the laws requiring pay rolls, bills and accounts to be certified shall have been complied with; and the commonwealth, its departments, officers, boards and commissions shall so pay every mechanic, workman and laborer employed by it or them, and every person employed in any other capacity by it or them in any penal or charitable institution, and every county and city shall so pay every employee engaged in its business the wag

Every person having employees in his service shall pay weekly or bi-weekly each such employee the wages earned by him to within six days of the termination of the pay period during which the wages were earned if employed for five or six days in a calendar week, or to within seven days of the termination of the pay period during which the wages were earned if such employee is employed seven days in a calendar week, or in the case of an employee who has worked for a period of less than five days, hereinafter called a casual employee, shall, within seven days after the termination of such period, pay the wages earned by such casual employee during such period, but any employee leaving his employment shall be paid in full on the following regular pay day, and, in the absence of a regular pay day, on the following Saturday; and any employee discharged from such employment shall be paid in full on the day of his discharge, or in Boston as soon as the laws requiring pay rolls, bills and accounts to be certified shall have been complied with; and the commonwealth, i
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the payment frequency from monthly to annually, which contradicts Massachusetts General Law Chapter 149 Section 148, as explained by the LLM. While the law specifically mentions 'employees' being paid weekly or bi-weekly, the Law Explanation argues that extending the payment period to annually for services rendered could be construed as a violation, especially if Domini depends on regular payments. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and Scraped Law Snippet 2 both state: 'Every person having employees in his service shall pay weekly or bi-weekly each such employee the wages earned by him.' Although Domini is a company, the LLM suggests that delaying payments significantly undermines the law's intent to ensure timely compensation for services rendered, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, the Sponsor shall not be deemed to have assumed, pursuant to this Agreement, any duties with respect to, and shall not be responsible for, the management of the Trust's assets or the rendering of investment advice and supervision with respect thereto or the distribution of shares of any series, nor shall the Sponsor be deemed to have assumed or have any responsibility with respect to functions specifically assumed by any transfer agent, custodian, fund accounting pricing agent or shareholder servicing agent of the Trust.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, the Sponsor shall not be deemed to have assumed, pursuant to this Agreement, any duties with respect to, and shall not be responsible for, the management of the Trust's assets or the rendering of investment advice and supervision with respect thereto or the distribution of shares of any series. The Sponsor shall be held responsible for functions specifically assumed by any transfer agent, custodian, fund accounting pricing agent or shareholder servicing agent of the Trust.
perturbation.explanation: Holding the sponsor responsible for the actions of the transfer agent, custodian, etc., directly contradicts the limited liability and specific roles defined in the Investment Company Act. These entities have separate contracts and obligations.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 17(f) outlines specific requirements for the custody of investment company assets and places responsibility on the custodian. By making the sponsor responsible for the custodian's actions, the modified text undermines the custodian's defined role and the protections intended by the Act, which assigns distinct responsibilities to different parties to prevent fraud and mismanagement. This creates potential conflicts of interest and blurring of responsibilities that the Investment Company Act seeks to avoid.

× No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 80a-17 - Transactions of certain affiliated persons and underwriters U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) Prohibited transactions It shall be unlawful for any affiliated person or promoter of or principal underwriter for a registered investment company (other than a company of the character described in section 80a–12(d)(3)(A) and (B) of this title), or any affiliated person of such a person, promoter, or principal underwriter, acting as principal— (1) knowingly to sell any security or other property to such registered company or to any company controlled by such registered company , unless such sale involves solely (A) securities of which the buyer is the issuer, (B) securities of which the seller is the issuer and which are part of a general offering to the holders of a class of its securities, or (C) securities deposited with the trustee of a unit investment trust or periodic payment plan by the depositor thereof; (2) knowingly to purchase from such registered company , or from any company controlled by such registered company , any security or other property (except securities of which the seller is the issuer) ; (3) to borrow money or other property from such registered company or from any company controlled by such registered company (unless the borrower is controlled by the lender) except as permitted in section 80a–21(b) of this title ; or (4) to loan money or other property to such registered company , or to any company controlled by such registered company , in contravention of such rules, regulations, or orders as the Commission may, after consultation with and taking into consideration the views of the Federal banking agencies (as defined in section 1813 of title 12 ), prescribe or issue consistent w
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the principle of assigning distinct responsibilities within investment companies, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. By holding the Sponsor responsible for the actions of the transfer agent, custodian, etc., the changed text undermines the independent obligations of these parties. While the scraped snippets (15 USC 80a-17) focus on transactions of affiliated persons and underwriters, the Perturbation and Law Explanations make it clear that this change creates conflicts of interest and blurring of responsibilities within an investment company, which the Investment Company Act seeks to avoid, thus creating a legal contradiction as the changed text increases the scope of liability for the sponsor.
